title: Non-uniqueness of Leray solutions of the forced Navier-Stokes equations

def:1.1.summary: Leray-Hopf solution: a divergence-free vector field u $\in L^\infty_t L^2_x \cap L^2_t$ Ḣ^1_x on $\mathbb{R}^3\times(0,T)$ that belongs to $C_w([0,T]$;$L^2)$, solves NSE distributionally, and satisfies the energy inequality (1.2).

thm:1.2.summary: Main non-uniqueness result (basic form): there exist T>0, body force $f\inL^1_t L^2_x$, and two distinct suitable Leray-Hopf solutions u, ū with zero initial data and the same force f. First proof that Leray-Hopf solutions of NSE are non-unique (with forcing).

thm:1.3.summary: Refined non-uniqueness: specifies the construction explicitly — a smooth compactly supported background profile Ū with force F̄ such that (a) linearized operator $L_ss$ has unstable eigenvalue $\lambda$ with smooth eigenfunction $\eta$, and (b) there exists $U^per$ on the unstable manifold with $||U^per||_{H^k}$ ≲ $e^{2\taua}$, giving two distinct suitable Leray-Hopf solutions ū and ū $+ u^lin + u^per$.

thm:2.1.summary: Unstable 2D vortex: there exists $m\ge2$ and smooth radially symmetric vorticity profile $\omega$̄$(\rho)$ with $|\omega$̄$| + \rho|\partial_\rho\omega$̄| ≲ ⟨$\rho$⟩^{-2} such that the linearized operator A on $L^2_m$ has an unstable eigenvalue $\lambda$ with $Re(\lambda)>0$. Adapts Vishik's 2D Euler instability to the NSE similarity setting.

prop:2.2.summary: Truncated unstable vortex: for an unstable vortex $\omega$̄ from thm:2.1 and $\lambda_\infty$ an unstable eigenvalue of A, for any $\varepsilon>0$ there exists R₀ such that for all $R\geR$₀, the truncated operator $A_R$ has an unstable eigenvalue $\lambda_R$ with $|\lambda_R - \lambda_\infty| < \varepsilon$.

cor:2.3.summary: The operator $L_\infty$ has an unstable eigenvalue. This is the key corollary of prop:2.2 in the limit ℓ$\to\infty$ that feeds into prop:2.6.

lem:2.4.summary: Divergence correction: for each ℓ$\ge2R$̄, there exists v_ℓ$\inC^\infty_0(B_{R̄}$;$\mathbb{R}^2)$ such that div_ℓ(ũ + v_ℓ) = 0, with v_ℓ $\to 0$ in $C^k(B_{R̄})$ as ℓ$\to\infty$. Corrects the approximate eigenvector to exactly satisfy the divergence-free condition.

lem:2.5.summary: Basic properties of operators K_ℓ, S_ℓ on $L^2_{\gamma$,ℓ}: K_ℓ is bounded and compact, S_ℓ is bounded with ||S_ℓ$||_{L^2_{\gamma$,ℓ$}\toL^2_\gamma} \to 0$ as ℓ$\to\infty$. These properties are essential for the Fredholm analysis in prop:2.2.

prop:2.6.summary: Axisymmetric 3D instability: for $\lambda_\infty$ an unstable eigenvalue of $L_\infty$ and any $\varepsilon>0$, there exists ℓ₀ such that for all ℓ$\ge$ℓ₀, the axisymmetric 3D linearized operator L_ℓ has an unstable eigenvalue $\lambda_$ℓ with $|\lambda_$ℓ $- \lambda_\infty| < \varepsilon$. Key $2D\to3D$ lifting of the instability.

lem:2.7.summary: Resolvent convergence: for compact K $\subset \rho(M_\infty + K_\infty) \cap {Re \lambda > 0}$, the resolvents $R(\lambda, M_$ℓ P_ℓ $+ K_\infty)$ converge to $R(\lambda, M_\infty + K_\infty)$ in $L^2_\gamma$ uniformly on K. Used in prop:2.6 to transfer eigenvalue from ℓ$=\infty$ to large ℓ.

thm:3.1.summary: Self-similar NSE instability (vorticity form): for $\lambda_\infty$ an unstable eigenvalue of $T_\infty$ with Re $\lambda_\infty > \mu$, for all $\varepsilon>0$ there exists $\beta$₀ such that for $\beta\ge\beta$₀, $T_\beta$ has unstable eigenvalue $\lambda_\beta$ with $|\lambda_\beta - \lambda_\infty| < \varepsilon$, and $L^{(\beta)}_vor$ has eigenvalue $\beta$̃$\lambda_\beta = \beta\lambda_\beta + 1/4$. Transfers 2D axisymmetric instability to self-similar 3D NSE vorticity operator.

cor:3.2.summary: Self-similar NSE instability (velocity form): the unstable eigenvalue $\beta$̃$\lambda_\beta$ of $L^{(\beta)}_vor$ from thm:3.1 is also an unstable eigenvalue of $L^{(\beta)}_vel$. Translates vorticity instability into velocity instability needed for thm:1.3.

lem:3.3.summary: Resolvent bound and convergence: $||R(\lambda, \beta$⁻^1D + M $+ S)||_{L^2_aps} \le (Re \lambda - \mu)$⁻^1, and $R(\lambda, \beta$⁻^1D + M $+ S) \toR(\lambda$, M + S) in $L^2$ as $\beta\to\infty$, locally uniformly in {Re $\lambda > \mu}$. Used in thm:3.1 to pass from finite $\beta$ to the $\beta\to\infty$ limit.

lem:3.4.summary: Resolvent convergence for $T_\beta$: uniform bounds $sup_\beta ||R(\lambda, T_\beta)|| < \infty$ on compact sets, and $R(\lambda, T_\beta)\Omega \toR(\lambda, T_\infty)\Omega$ in $L^2 uniformly-in-\lambda$. Essential for eigenvalue perturbation theory in thm:3.1.

thm:4.1.summary: Non-uniqueness criterion: given Ū compactly supported smooth divergence-free and $L_ss$ having an unstable eigenvalue $\lambda$, then the maximally unstable eigenvalue exists (Re $\lambda =$ sup Re $\sigma(L_ss) > 0)$ and the unstable manifold perturbation $U^per$ of thm:1.3 part (b) exists. Converts the existence of ANY unstable eigenvalue into the maximally unstable one needed for the construction.

lem:4.2.summary: Spectral bound for $L_ss$: the growth bound $\omega$₀$(L_ss) = max{\omega_ess(L_ss)$, $s(L_ss)}$, and the spectrum in {Re $\lambda > \omega_ess}$ is finite with finite-rank projections. Used to establish maximality of the unstable eigenvalue in thm:4.1.

prop:4.3.summary: Maximally unstable eigenvalue: if a $= s(L_ss) > 0$ then a $< \infty$, and there exist eigenvalue $\lambda =$ a+ib and smooth eigenfunction $\eta$ with $L_ss \eta = \lambda\eta$. Semigroup bound: $||e^{\tau L_ss}$ U₀$||_{L^2} \leM(\delta) e^{\tau(a+\delta)} ||U$₀$||_{L^2}$ for any $\delta>0$. The eigenfunction $\eta$ enters the construction of $u^lin$.

lem:4.4.summary: Semigroup smoothing: $||e^{\tau L_ss}$ U₀$||_{H^{\sigma₂}} \le$ M $\tau^{-(\sigma$₂$-\sigma$₁$)/2} e^{\tau(a+\delta)} ||U$₀$||_{H^{\sigma₁}}$ for $\sigma$₂$\ge\sigma$₁$\ge0$. Provides the high-regularity estimates needed for the fixed-point argument in prop:4.7.

prop:4.5.summary: Unstable manifold existence: for $a=s(L_ss)>0$ and N>5/2, there exist T, $\varepsilon$₀>0 and $U^per \inC((-\infty,T]$; $H^N(\mathbb{R}^3))$ solving the nonlinear NSE perturbation equation with $||U^per(\cdot,\tau)||_{H^N} \le e^{(a+\varepsilon₀)\tau}$ for all $\tau\leT$. This is part (b) of thm:1.3.

rem:4.6.summary: Notes that existence of $U^per$ could follow from abstract semilinear parabolic theory (Henry's book), but the explicit construction given generalizes to non-compactly supported backgrounds (see rem:4.8).

prop:4.7.summary: Contraction for the unstable manifold map: the fixed-point map T is a contraction on ${U\inX$ : ||U||_X $\le 1}$, proving existence of $U^per$. T maps the ball to itself (4.22) and is contractive, establishing prop:4.5 via Banach fixed-point theorem.

rem:4.8.summary: Extension to non-compactly supported backgrounds: general Ū with natural decay $|\nabla^k$ Ū$(\xi)|$ ≲ ⟨$\xi$⟩^{-k-1} can be treated using the modified ansatz ũ(x,t) = ū$(x)\chi(x/t^\alpha)$, introducing forcing and lower-order terms handled perturbatively.

edges.note: thm:1.2 basic non-uniqueness follows from the refined thm:1.3 which specifies the explicit construction with Ū, $\eta$, and $U^per$
thm:1.3 part (b) (existence of $U^per)$ follows from thm:4.1 given part $(a) (L_ss$ has an unstable eigenvalue); thm:4.1 handles the passage to the maximally unstable eigenvalue
thm:1.3 part $(a) (L_ss$ has an unstable eigenvalue) is established via thm:3.1 (self-similar NSE vorticity instability) and cor:3.2 (velocity form)
cor:3.2 (velocity version of instability) directly provides the unstable eigenvalue of $L^{(\beta)}_vel = L_ss$ needed for thm:1.3 part (a)
thm:4.1 uses prop:4.3 to find the maximally unstable eigenvalue a $= s(L_ss)$ and eigenfunction $\eta$ from any unstable eigenvalue
thm:4.1 conclusion (part b of thm:1.3) is proved by prop:4.5 (unstable manifold existence)
prop:4.3 uses lem:4.2 spectral theory for $L_ss$ to establish the finiteness of $s(L_ss)$ and the existence of the maximally unstable eigenvalue
prop:4.3 semigroup smoothing lem:4.4 provides the $H^{\sigma₂}$ bounds from $H^{\sigma₁}$ data needed throughout Section 4
prop:4.5 existence of $U^per$ is proved via the contraction in prop:4.7 (Banach fixed-point theorem for the nonlinear map T)
prop:4.7 contraction estimate uses the semigroup bounds from prop:$4.3 (||e^{\tau L_ss}||_{L^2} \le$ M $e^{\tau(a+\delta)})$ to bound the fixed-point map T
prop:4.7 uses smoothing lem:4.4 in the definition of the X-norm (and $H^N$ estimates for T) to close the contraction
thm:3.1 proof uses the resolvent bound and convergence from lem:3.3 to show $\beta$⁻^1D + M + S $\to$ M + S resolvent-wise, enabling eigenvalue perturbation theory
thm:3.1 uses lem:3.4 resolvent convergence $T_\beta \to T_\infty$ to transfer the unstable eigenvalue from $\beta\to\infty$ limit to large finite $\beta$
thm:3.1 uses the unstable 2D vortex $\omega$̄ from thm:2.1 as the initial construction from which the self-similar NSE instability is built
cor:3.2 (velocity eigenvalue) follows directly from thm:3.1 (vorticity eigenvalue) by the algebraic relation between $L^{(\beta)}_vor$ and $L^{(\beta)}_vel$
thm:2.1 proof uses prop:2.6 (axisymmetric 3D L_ℓ has unstable eigenvalue) to establish the full 3D axisymmetric instability
thm:2.1 proof uses prop:2.2 to pass from the infinite-domain operator A to the truncated $A_R$ with controlled eigenvalue
prop:2.6 uses lem:2.4 to correct the divergence of the approximate eigenfunction, ensuring the eigenfunction for L_ℓ is exactly divergence-free
prop:2.6 uses the resolvent convergence lem:2.7 to transfer the unstable eigenvalue from ℓ$=\infty (cor$:2.3) to large finite ℓ
prop:2.6 uses the compactness of K_ℓ and decay of S_ℓ from lem:2.5 in the Fredholm theory for L_ℓ
cor:$2.3 (L_\infty$ unstable eigenvalue) uses the compactness of $K_\infty$ from lem:2.5 in the Fredholm alternative to find the unstable spectrum
prop:2.2 uses boundedness and compactness of K_ℓ from lem:2.5 in the spectral approximation argument

leray_hopf_setup.description: Definition of Leray-Hopf solutions and statement of the non-uniqueness theorems

2d_vortex_instability.description: Construct an unstable 2D Vishik-type vortex profile and lift it to axisymmetric 3D instability

3d_nse_instability.description: Transfer the 2D axisymmetric instability to the full self-similar 3D NSE linearized operators (vorticity and velocity forms)

unstable_manifold_construction.description: Use the unstable eigenvalue to construct the nonlinear perturbation on the unstable manifold via fixed-point theorem

remarks_and_extensions.description: Remarks on abstract alternatives and generalization to non-compactly supported backgrounds

BuckmasterVicolAnnals.title: Nonuniqueness of weak solutions to the Navier-Stokes equation
BuckmasterVicolAnnals.note: Parallel non-uniqueness result using convex integration; ABC's mechanism (Vishik instability) is completely different

IsettOnsager.title: A proof of Onsager's conjecture
IsettOnsager.note: Cited as background for the convex integration / non-uniqueness landscape

cheskidov2020sharp.title: Sharp nonuniqueness for the Navier-Stokes equations
cheskidov2020sharp.note: Parallel non-uniqueness result; CL uses convex integration for sharp LPS-threshold non-uniqueness, ABC uses Vishik instability for Leray-Hopf

buckmaster2021non.title: Non-conservative H^{1/2} weak solutions of the incompressible 3D Euler equations
buckmaster2021non.note: Cited as related convex integration result for non-conservative Euler solutions

BuckmasterColomboVicol.title: Wild solutions of the Navier-Stokes equations whose singular sets in time have Hausdorff dimension strictly less than 1
BuckmasterColomboVicol.note: Colombo is co-author here; BCV constructs non-Leray wild solutions, ABC constructs Leray-Hopf non-uniqueness. Related program.

Vishik1.title: Instability and non-uniqueness in the Cauchy problem for the Euler equations. Part I
Vishik1.note: Primary predecessor — Vishik's unstable 2D vortex construction is adapted in Section 2 of this paper

Vishik2.title: Instability and non-uniqueness in the Cauchy problem for the Euler equations. Part II
Vishik2.note: Second part of Vishik's instability construction

jiasverakselfsim.title: Local-in-space estimates near initial time for weak solutions of the Navier-Stokes equations and forward self-similar solutions
jiasverakselfsim.note: Jia-Šverák predicted the non-uniqueness mechanism: if self-similar NSE has an unstable perturbation, then non-uniqueness follows. ABC proves this prediction.

OurLectureNotes.title: Instability and nonuniqueness for the 2D Euler equations in vorticity form, after M. Vishik
OurLectureNotes.note: Companion paper by the same group explaining Vishik's 2D instability in detail
